coredata.dc:description: Conventional methodologies for the detection and remediation of hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)) are characterized by their complexity and financial burden, necessitating the pursuit of methodologies that are both efficacious and economical, with minimal environmental impact. Within this investigation, we report the fabrication of hollow polysulfone (PSF) microspheres, imbued with bifluorescent quantum dots and metal-organic frameworks, utilizing a Pickering emulsion paradigm to facilitate photolytic Cr(VI) degradation and consistent visual detection. Our results reveal that these engineered microspheres sustain an exceptional degradation efficiency exceeding 98 % for Cr(VI) over quintuple cycles, devoid of agitation, alongside pronounced fluorescence quenching indicative of a durable and recyclable sensing modality. The inherent low density and vacuous architecture of these spheres afford them buoyancy, augmenting their interaction with aqueous pollutants. Furthermore, the material exhibits a distinguished selectivity for Cr(VI), markedly eclipsing alternative metal ions in fluorescence suppression assays, validating its efficacy for heavy metal detection. The dual-functional attributes, coupled with their enduring stability and eco-friendly profile, position the CCUP microspheres at the forefront of water treatment innovation, potentially revolutionizing the real-time tracing of heavy metal pollutants.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Conventional methodologies for the detection and remediation of hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)) are characterized by their complexity and financial burden, necessitating the pursuit of methodologies that are both efficacious and economical, with minimal environmental impact. Within this investigation, we report the fabrication of hollow polysulfone (PSF) microspheres, imbued with bifluorescent quantum dots and metal-organic frameworks, utilizing a Pickering emulsion paradigm to facilitate photolytic Cr(VI) degradation and consistent visual detection. Our results reveal that these engineered microspheres sustain an exceptional degradation efficiency exceeding 98 % for Cr(VI) over quintuple cycles, devoid of agitation, alongside pronounced fluorescence quenching indicative of a durable and recyclable sensing modality. The inherent low density and vacuous architecture of these spheres afford them buoyancy, augmenting their interaction with aqueous pollutants. Furthermore, the material exhibits a distinguished selectivity for Cr(VI), markedly eclipsing alternative metal ions in fluorescence suppression assays, validating its efficacy for heavy metal detection. The dual-functional attributes, coupled with their enduring stability and eco-friendly profile, position the CCUP microspheres at the forefront of water treatment innovation, potentially revolutionizing the real-time tracing of heavy metal pollutants.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The rapid growth of the global economy and industrialization has heightened concerns about environmental pollution, posing serious challenges to human health and ecosystems [1–4]. Heavy metal pollution, i particularly hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)), draws significant attention due to its persistence and toxicity [5]. Cr(VI), commonly originating from industrial processes such as electroplating, leather processing, and chemical manufacturing, is highly toxic and carcinogenic [6,7]. It accumulates in living organisms, causing severe health issues through the food chain [8]. The U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (USEPA) mandates a maximum Cr(VI) level of 100 μg/L in drinking water [9]. Therefore, precise quantification of Cr(VI) is imperative to safeguard drinking water and ensure the well-being of biological entities.

Developing efficient, rapid, and accurate technologies for Cr(VI) detection and degradation is crucial for protecting aquatic environments and maintaining ecological balance. Traditional detection methods, such as chemical and spectroscopic analyses [10–13], are sensitive and accurate but are hindered by complexity, lengthy processes, and high costs. Similarly, conventional Cr(VI) degradation methods, like chemical oxidation and reduction precipitation, are costly and require harsh conditions, posing risks of secondary pollution. Various substrates have been explored for chromogenic and fluorometric detection of Cr(VI), including organic compounds [14], metallic nanoparticles [15], semiconducting quantum dots [16], and metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) [17]. Despite their proficiency, these agents often face challenges like complex synthesis, high costs, limited biocompatibility, and potential secondary contamination [18]. Integrating chromogenic and fluorometric detection in a single assay remains unresolved, necessitating the development of novel optical materials that are cost-effective, biocompatible, and eco-friendly.

Quantum dots, known for their size-dependent properties, exceptional photoelectric performance, and chemical stability, are indispensable in fields like biomedicine, optoelectronics, and sensing [19–23]. CdTe quantum dots (CdTeQDs) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are notable for their superior optical properties and chemical stability. However, challenges such as aggregation, poor light stability, and narrow absorption range limit their environmental applications. To address these issues, researchers have explored loading quantum dots onto carriers like MOFs, which offer large surface areas, controlled pore structures, and unique physicochemical properties [24]. MOFs materials have unique performance advantages such as controllable pore size, modified pore surface, low density, and high specific surface area, making this new type of porous material widely used in fields like adsorption and photocatalysis [25–28]. MOFs, such as UiO-66-NH2, are distinguished by large pores, high surface area, and functional groups, making them ideal for adsorption, catalysis, and separation [29–31]. Building on previous research, we synthesized UiO-66@PSF porous microspheres augmented with CQDs using a novel Pickering emulsion template method. This fabrication enhances the photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline and methylene blue under visible light, due to the microspheres' unique hollow structure, which increases the surface area [32]. MOF-impregnated PSF microspheres exhibit superior adsorptive and photocatalytic properties for organic substances, attributed to their low density, heightened porosity, and reusability [33]. Using UiO-66-NH2 within porous aerogels shows immense promise for revolutionizing wastewater treatment methodologies [34,35]. Pickering emulsion, characterized by robust stability and eco-friendly attributes, involve colloidal particles anchoring at the interface of two immiscible liquids [36]. These colloidal particles act as surfactants, encapsulating oil droplets within a particulate mantle, forming a barrier at the oil-water interface. The internal phase volume fraction of the emulsion can be freely adjusted, allowing efficient synthesis of functional materials like Janus particles, foams, and hollow structures [32,37]. MOF particles, due to their amphiphilicity, are efficient for preparing and applying Pickering emulsions [38–41].

In this research, we developed a series of CdTeQD-CQD@UiO-66-NH2/PSF (CCUP) composite materials using Pickering emulsion, designed for visible-light-driven photocatalytic degradation of Cr(VI) and continuous visual detection. T Incorporating CQDs onto UiO-66-NH2 enhances light utilization and electron-hole separation, generating higher yields of free radicals for oxidative degradation. Integrating CdTeQDs onto UiO-66-NH2 endows the composite with Cr(VI) detection capabilities, offering a simpler alternative to traditional methods. Utilizing a Pickering emulsion template method, the quantum dot-loaded UiO-66-NH2 was embedded onto porous and hollow PSF microspheres, forming the advanced CCUP. This material leverages the optical properties of CdTeQDs and CQDs and the structural characteristics of UiO-66-NH2 and PSF, presenting innovative approaches for detecting and degrading Cr(VI).

Reagents employed in this study included citric acid, polysulfone (PSF), dichloromethane (DCM, ≥99.5 %), polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP), ethylenediamine, polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), methylene blue, zirconium chloride (ZrCl4, 97 %), 1-methyl-2-pyrrolidinone (NMP, ≥ 99.5 %), anhydrous ethanol (EtOH), aminoterephthalic acid (NH2-BDC, 98 %), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF, 98 %), N-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-N′-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), all procured from Macklin (Shanghai, China). Solution preparation was executed using ultrapure water with a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ·cm, sourced from FLEX4, ElgaPURELAB, UK. The aforementioned reagents were of analytical grade and necessitated no additional purification processes.

The creation of the compound UiO-66-NH2 was achieved through a solvothermal method [42,43]. An initial admixture of 0.65 g of ZrCl4 and 0.51 g of NH2-BDC was homogenized in 50 mL of DMF, followed by 15 minutes of ultrasonic agitation. Subsequent to the introduction of 10 mL of acetic acid, the blend was stirred continuously for a duration of two hours. The resultant concoction was then relocated to a high-pressure autoclave and subjected to a temperature of 130°C for a full diurnal cycle. The post-reaction yield was cleansed in a sequenced manner with DMF and anhydrous ethanol, and then subjected to a drying phase at 100°C over a 12-hour period. In a separate procedure for synthesizing CQDs, 5 g of citric acid and 2.5 g of ethylenediamine were dissolved in 50 mL of deionized water in a round-bottom flask. This solution was heated to 160°C with stirring for 6 hours. After cooling, the solution was purified by dialysis in deionized water for 48 hours, with water changes every two hours, using a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cutoff of 1 k. The purified solution was then freeze-dried to produce amine-functionalized CQDs.

We refined established methods from the literature [44] to synthesize CdTe quantum dots, incorporating several modifications. Initially, 40.0 mL of a 0.02 mol/L CdCl2 solution was introduced into a three-necked flask, where the pH was adjusted to 11.5 using a 2.0 mol/L sodium hydroxide solution. The solution underwent nitrogen purging for 20 minutes. Subsequently, a designated volume of NaHTe was rapidly dissolved and injected into the vigorously stirred solution. The stoichiometric ratio of Cd2+:TGA:Te- was meticulously maintained at 1:2:0.25. This CdTe precursor was then transferred to a polytetrafluoroethylene autoclave and subjected to heating at 140°C for a predetermined duration, resulting in the formation of CdTe fluorescent quantum dots. Following synthesis, these quantum dots were purified through repeated centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 5 minutes using methanol triple the volume of the sample, aimed at removing unreacted precursors such as Cd+. The resulting precipitate was dried under vacuum and subsequently redissolved in 40 mL of distilled water for storage at 4°C. In the subsequent phase, the CCU was fabricated by incorporating dual fluorescent materials during the synthesis of ZIF-8. Specifically, a mixture of 2-methylimidazole (16.0 mmol), 1.7 mL of InPQDs (50 nmol/L), 120 μg of CDs, and 30 mL of methanol was stirred for one hour. Zn(NO3)2·6 H2O (1.0 mmol) was gradually added to the mixture, which was then stirred for an additional eight hours and allowed to stand for two hours. The product was collected by centrifugation and dried under vacuum at 50°C. The final stage involved the incorporation of 0.2 wt% CQD and 0.1 wt% CdTeQD, relative to UiO-66-NH2, into a 1 wt% UiO-66-NH2 aqueous solution. The mixture was stirred magnetically for 24 hours in the presence of EDC/NHS (at 0.1 wt% and 0.3 wt% respective to UiO-66-NH2) to facilitate bonding. Excess reactants were removed by multiple centrifugations with deionized water at 1000 rpm. The resulting product, named CCU-x, varies in mass ratio from 0.2 to 1.0, presenting a tailored approach to enhanced photocatalytic activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of CCUP microspheres
ce:section.ce:para: The production of CCUP microspheres was carefully carried out using the Pickering emulsion evaporation technique. A clear solution was made by dissolving 0.125 g of PSF and 0.1 g of PVP in 6.0 milliliters of a mixed solvent of NMP and DCM at a ratio of 1–5. To this solution, 0.11 g of CCUs containing various amounts of CQDs, from 0.2 to 1.0 wt%, were added. The solution was stirred for 30 minutes to fully dissolve all components. This solution was then carefully injected into a coagulation bath with 50 mL of a 1 % PVA solution through microchannels, leading to the creation of composite hollow microspheres made of CCUP. The system was stirred at room temperature for five hours to ensure proper formation. After stirring, the microspheres were collected using a fine sieve with 500 meshes, thoroughly washed with ethanol and distilled water, and filtered to remove any loosely bound CCU nanoparticles. The final product was dried at 60°C and labeled as CCUP-x, where 'x' represents the weight ratio of CQD to UiO-66-NH2, which varied from 0.2 to 1.0. For comparison, PSF microspheres without CCU and those containing only UiO-66-NH2 were also produced using the same method.

The analysis of the microspheres' structure and composition employed a variety of advanced techniques. Their surface morphology was examined with a Hitachi Regulus 8220 scanning electron microscope (SEM). The fluorescence characteristics of the CCUP-x microspheres were evaluated using a Leica SP8-X confocal laser scanning microscope. To prepare for this evaluation, 5 mL of the microspheres were dispersed in 8 mL of water, and a 1 mL sample of this dispersion was placed onto a microscope slide and covered with a glass coverslip. The CCUP-x microspheres displayed two distinct excitation peaks at wavelengths of 455 and 629 nm. The elemental makeup of the microsphere surfaces was analyzed using energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS). The crystalline structure was probed with a Bruker D8Advance X-ray diffractometer, collecting X-ray diffraction patterns over a range of 5° to 90°. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), conducted with a Bruker Vertex-V70 instrument, was used to identify the functional groups in the CCUP. To ensure accuracy, each of these analyses was performed three times per sample, with the results averaged. The microspheres' porosity was determined using the classical gravimetric method, and their density was calculated using a specific equation. Each sample underwent three separate tests to verify the results' reproducibility. Porosity was calculated according to the gravimetric method as outlined in a given formula:

P represent the masses of CCU and PSF in the composite microspheres. The densities of water, PSF, and UiO66-NH2 are given as ρW (1.0 g/cm³), ρP (1.24 g/cm³), and ρM (0.82 g/cm³), respectively. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Escalab 250Xi, Thermo Scientific, USA) was used to analyze the chemical composition of the CCU. The optical characteristics of CCUP were examined using a PerkinElmer Lambda 950 UV/Vis spectrophotometer and a Hitachi F4600 fluorescence spectrometer. Photoelectrochemical properties of CCUP were evaluated with a CS310 electrochemical workstation from China. For active substance capture experiments and electron spin resonance (ESR) analysis, tert-butyl alcohol (TBA) was utilized to scavenge ·OH radicals, with sodium ethylenediaminetetraacetate (EDTA) and 1,4-benzoquinone (BQ) used for ·O2- scavenging. The impact of these active substances on MB removal by CUP-x was studied through scavenging of ·O2-. Spin traps included 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO), and ESR spectra under simulated sunlight conditions were recorded with a Bruker EMX Plus to detect ·OH and ·O2- produced by CUP-x/H2O2. To detect the formation of TEMPN DMPO-·O2- and DMPO-·OH, dissolve 10 mg of CUP in 0.5 mL deionized water containing 45 μL DMPO under simulated sunlight.

Mercury intrusion porosimetry is the preferred technique for analyzing large pores or the spaces between particles in various materials, especially when nitrogen adsorption methods are inadequate [32]. It plays a vital role in measuring the saturation volume during the absorption of fluids by materials. The structure of CCUP-x microspheres was meticulously measured using a Micromeritics 9620 mercury intrusion porosimeter. To prepare for this procedure, it is critical to eliminate all moisture and water vapor from the sample, which is achieved by heating the sample in an oven at 105°C for at least 24 hours. The samples used for mercury intrusion porosimetry are generally around 0.7 g, or 0.3 cm3 in volume. The porosimeter can exert pressures up to 414 megapascals (MPa) and is sensitive enough to identify pore sizes down to 5 nanometers.

Disperse 20 mg of CCU in 10 mL of HEPES buffer solution (0.06 mol/L, pH 8.0) and sonicate for 2 minutes. To examine the interaction between CCU and Cr(VI), add 100 μL of this CCU solution (2 mg/mL) to 1.5 mL of the same HEPES buffer. Then introduce varying concentrations (0–10 mg/L) of Cr(VI) to the mixture. Adjust the total volume to 2 mL with additional HEPES buffer. After stirring the solution at room temperature for 3 minutes, excite at 360 nm and record the emission spectrum. Collect the fluorescence emission intensities at 629 nm and 455 nm to analyze their relative ratios.

To evaluate the efficiency of photocatalytic degradation of Cr(VI) by CCUP, UiO-66-NH2, and PSF, we employed the XPA-7 photocatalytic apparatus from Nanjing Xujiang Electromechanical Plant. The experiments were conducted at ambient conditions. A 350 W xenon lamp, situated 8 centimeters from a reflecting surface, provided the necessary light for the reduction of Cr(VI). The setup was equipped with a UV filter that blocked wavelengths below 420 nm. Initially, 30 mg of the selected photocatalyst was submerged in 40 mL of a Cr(VI) solution with a concentration of 20 milligrams per liter. This mixture was agitated in the absence of light for half an hour to ensure that adsorption equilibrium was attained. Subsequently, the solution was irradiated with UV light from the xenon lamp to commence the photocatalytic process. At 15-minute intervals, 1.0 mL samples were extracted from the mixture. These samples were then spun down in a centrifuge. The concentration of Cr(VI) remaining after each interval was quantified using a UV–visible spectrophotometer. The rate at which Cr(VI) was degraded was determined by applying an equation referenced as formula (3):

Degradation = ( C −
C

(3)

where, Cr
0 and C represent the concentrations of the dye at the start of irradiation (t=0) and at a given time t, respectively. CCUP-x microspheres, containing CQD levels of 0.2 wt%, 0.5 wt%, and 1.0 wt% relative to UiO-66-NH2, were utilized as photocatalysts in a 40 mL aqueous Cr(VI) solution (20 mg/L), under simulated sunlight conditions for the photocatalytic degradation of Cr(VI). The light source was a 350 W xenon lamp fitted with an infrared cutoff filter (λ>800 nm). Prior to exposure to light, the solution was stirred in darkness for 30 minutes to achieve adsorption-desorption equilibrium between the photocatalyst and Cr(VI). Samples from the treated solution were collected every 15 minutes and the concentration of Cr(VI) was measured at 560 nm using an ultraviolet-visible spectrophotometer. Following the photocatalytic reaction, CCUP microspheres were separated by centrifugation, thoroughly rinsed with deionized water to eliminate any remaining impurities, and dried for reuse in further experiments.

Scheme 1 provides a schematic illustration of the fabrication process of the CCUP composite materials and highlights their dual functionality in the photocatalytic degradation and fluorescence-based detection of hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)). Initially, a precursor solution is prepared by stirring together CQD, CdTeQD, and UiO-66-NH2 in a solvent mixture of NMP and DCM. This mixture is then used to coat PSF microspheres using Pickering emulsion tamplate method. The process culminates with the formation of porous and hollow microspheres upon solvent evaporation. These CCUP microspheres fulfill a dual purpose: in the presence of visible light at an oil/water interface, they efficiently catalyze the photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) to Cr(III), evidenced by a change in the solution color. Simultaneously, leveraging the intrinsic fluorescence properties of the quantum dots, the microspheres display fluorescence quenching in the presence of Cr(VI), thereby enabling real-time and continuous detection. Pictorial insets within the figure vividly demonstrate the significant visual contrast in both the solution color and fluorescence intensity before and after treatment, effectively showcasing the CCUP microspheres' potential in environmental remediation and monitoring.

In the TEM image shown in Fig. 1a, the typical irregular polyhedral form of CCU is visible, indicative of its crystalline nature. The image with increased magnification clearly illustrates CQDs and CdTeQDs uniformly dispersed on the surface of UiO-66-NH2, suggesting potential synergistic interactions among CQDs, CdTeQDs, and the MOF scaffolds. Fig. S1 presents low-magnification, medium-magnification, and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images of CCU nanoparticles. The HRTEM image reveals the lattice fringes of the CQDs and CdTeQDs, with spacings of 0.34 nm (002) and 0.22 nm (110), respectively. These detailed images confirm the successful synthesis and integration of CQDs and CdTeQDs within the UiO-66-NH2 framework, supporting the structural integrity and composition of the CCU nanoparticles. Fig. 1b presents the FT-IR spectrum of CQD, highlighting its N-H characteristic peak at 867 cm−1, which also appears in the spectrum of CCU. However, the peak at 2922 cm−1, characteristic of CQD, nearly vanishes in the CCU spectrum, indicating that CQD has reacted with UiO-66-NH2 and is successfully anchored on its surface. Furthermore, the retention of two characteristic peaks of CdTeQDs at 1630 cm−1 and 1387 cm−1 in the CCU spectrum confirms their successful integration with UiO-66-NH2. The crystallinity of UiO-66-NH2 and its nanocomposites with CQDs and CdTeQDs was verified by X-ray diffraction, as shown in Fig. 1c. All characteristic diffraction peaks of UiO‑66‑NH2 align well with the simulated peaks. Peaks at 7.3, 8.4, and 25.6°, corresponding to the (111), (002), and (006) planes, respectively. respectively, demonstrate that the addition of CQD and CdTeQDs has negligible impact on the crystal structure of UiO-66-NH2.

Additionally, CCU exhibits distinctive fluorescence emission spectral characteristics, as depicted in Fig. 1d. When comparing the fluorescence emission spectra of CQDs, CdTeQDs, CQDs/CdTeQDs, and UiO-66-NH2, it is evident that the emission peaks of CCU at 455 and 629 nm align with those of CQDs and CdTeQDs, respectively. In contrast, pure UiO-66-NH2 does not exhibit any significant fluorescence emission within this range. These observations confirm that the fluorescence of CCU originates specifically from the CQDs and CdTeQDs, and importantly, these properties are preserved within the UiO-66-NH2 framework. Further tests, involving thorough rinsing of CCU with deionized water followed by centrifugation to obtain a supernatant for fluorescence testing, showed a nearly flat-line intensity (represented by a green line in the figure). This result substantiates that all CdTeQDs and CQDs are effectively encapsulated within the pores of UiO-66-NH2, without any residual presence in the solution, thus indicating the successful synthesis of CCU. Notably, the significant wavelength separation of 204 nm between the dual emission peaks (455 and 629 nm) in CCU minimizes interference between them during the fluorescence detection process, enhancing the suitability of this material for practical applications using dual-emission frequency probes. Moreover, under excitation wavelengths ranging from 310 to 360 nm, CCU consistently demonstrates stable emission at 455 and 629 nm (Fig. 1e), highlighting that the UiO-66-NH2 crystal structure imposes substantial constraints on the positioning and interaction of CQDs and CdTeQDs.

XPS analysis (Fig. f-i) provides crucial insights into the elemental composition, valence states, and chemical environments of the CCU material. Fig. 1g illustrates the C 1 s spectra for both UiO-66-NH2 and CCU. The C 1 s spectrum of CCU exhibits three distinct peaks at 284.3 eV (C-C), 285.8 eV (C-O), and 288.3 eV (CO). The integration of CQDs and CdTeQDs into the composite slightly raises the binding energies for C-C and C-O, while the CO peak shifts to a lower energy, indicating interactions between the CQDs and the functional groups within UiO-66-NH2. Fig. 1g highlights these shifts, suggesting changes in the electronic environment due to this interaction. Additionally, Fig. 1h shows the O 1 s spectrum of CCU, which presents three peaks at 530.0 eV, 531.4 eV, and 532.0 eV, corresponding to lattice oxygen (Olat), adsorbed oxygen (Oads), and surface hydroxyl groups (OOH), respectively [45]. Notably, while the peaks for Olattice and Oads remain unchanged, the peak for OOH exhibits a blue shift, implying that the incorporation of CQDs may influence water adsorption characteristics. Lastly, the Zr 3d spectrum of UiO-66-NH2, shown in Fig. 1i, is deconvoluted into two peaks at 182.3 eV (3d5/2) and 1847.eV(3d3/2) [46], confirming that the addition of CQDs and CdTeQDs does not significantly alter the chemical environment surrounding the zirconium. Table S1 details the elemental composition and corresponding binding energies for UiO-66-NH2 and CCU-1.0. The binding energies for O 1 s can be observed at 529.5 eV (lattice oxygen), 531.2 eV (adsorbed oxygen), and 533.1 eV (hydroxyl groups), with a roughly equivalent atomic percentage of 15 % in both materials. The binding energies are found at 284.2 eV (C-C), 285.6 eV (C-O), and 288.2 eV (CO) for the C 1 s, with the atomic percentage decreasing noticeably from 70.8 % in UiO-66-NH2 to 48 % in CCU-1.0, thus indicating the addition of other elements. The binding energy for N 1 s is ∼400 eV, and the atomic percentage slightly increased from 6.7 % to 7 % in CCU-1. The binding energies of the Zr 3d are localized at 182.3 eV (3d5/2) and 184.7 eV (3d3/2), in which atomic percentage increases from 3.6 % to 9 %, respectively, with an increased content of zirconium in the composite. In addition, the presence of Te 3d and Cd 3d at ∼576 eV and ∼405 eV, respectively, with the atomic percentage of 1.7 % and 3.9 % in CCU-1.0, also confirmed the doping of tellurium and cadmium to the composite.

Under optimal conditions, the performance of the CCUP nanosensors for detecting Cr(VI) was thoroughly investigated using the fluorescence titration method, with results displayed in Fig. 2a and Fig. S2. Upon introducing varying amounts of Cr(VI), there was a notable decrease in the fluorescence intensity at 629 nm, while the intensity at 455 nm remained relatively constant. This behavior suggests that the nanocomposite sensor can effectively distinguish Cr(VI) through fluorescent colorimetric detection. The relationship between the ratio of fluorescence intensities I455/I629 and the Cr(VI) concentration demonstrates a strong linear correlation within a range of 0–10 mg/L (I455/I620=0.07381C Cr(VI)+0.5626) with R2= 0.995 (Fig. 2b). This multicolor fluorescent nanosensor, compared to traditional single-signal response probes, offers built-in self-calibration and provides a higher level of visualization for Cr(VI) detection. The enhanced sensitivity of the CCUP nanosensors is primarily attributed to the MOF's porous structure which not only aids in the uniform dispersion and stabilization of CQDs and CdTeQDs but also increases the interaction area with the analyte due to its extensive surface area [47].

The selectivity of the CCUP nanosensors for Cr(VI) was rigorously tested, particularly against common metal ions at a concentration of 5 mg/L under optimal experimental conditions, with the findings depicted in Fig. 2c. It is observed that Cr(VI) induces a substantial quenching of fluorescence, while Fe3+ ions cause moderate quenching. Other metal cations have minimal or no impact on fluorescence intensity, highlighting the high selectivity of CCUP for Cr(VI). These results underscore the efficacy of this dual fluorescent quantum dot probe in the specific determination of Cr(VI), further affirming its superiority in detecting this particular metal ion with high accuracy and selectivity.

3.2. Characterization of CCUP microspheres
Fig. 3a-d showcase a comprehensive set of SEM images that depict the surface morphology of CCUP microspheres, denoted as CCUP-x. Here, "x" indicates different mass fractions of CQDs incorporated into the CCU nanoparticles, in relation to the metal-organic framework UiO-66-NH2. These specific fractions function as stabilizers within the Pickering emulsion framework. These fractions act as stabilizing agents in the Pickering emulsion structure. The control sample, designated as Ctrl, shows a relatively smooth PSF surface with sporadic pores. Increasing the CQD concentration from CCUP-0.2 to CCUP-1.0 does not disrupt the microspheres' porosity or roughness, demonstrating the gentle integration of the CCU nanoparticles into the microsphere structure. Notably, Fig. 3c highlights the dense population of nanoparticles at the oil-water interface, which suggests a robust nanoparticle coating on the exterior of the microspheres. This coating is crucial for the dual functionality of the microspheres, which includes photocatalytic degradation under visible light and real-time fluorescence detection of contaminants. Furthermore, Fig. 3d reveals the hollow structure of the CCUP-1.0 variant, which not only ensures a high surface-to-volume ratio favorable for effective contaminant interaction but also highlights the material’s lower density, beneficial for recovery and reuse in aqueous systems. The hollow structure of the microspheres greatly enhances their photocatalytic efficiency. This design allows for an efficient exchange of reactants and products inside the sphere and provides a large surface area for the adsorption of pollutants [48]. Furthermore, the compact arrangement of CCU nanoparticles on the PSF microsphere surfaces boosts photocatalytic activity by expanding the available surface area for pollutant adsorption, thereby increasing the overall efficiency of the photocatalytic processes [49]. These SEM images not only confirm the successful synthesis and uniform surface composition of the CCUP microspheres but also demonstrate their potential to enhance both optical and catalytic properties, crucial for advancing environmental cleanup technologies, especially in monitoring and treating hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)).

Fig. 3e-g provide a comprehensive characterization of the CCUP-1.0 microspheres, utilizing EDS and CLSM to evaluate their elemental composition and fluorescence properties. The EDS image in Fig. 3e shows the uniform distribution of sulfur (S), cadmium (Cd), and tellurium (Te) within a single microsphere, confirming the homogeneous integration of CdTe quantum dots. This uniformity is crucial for ensuring consistent photocatalytic and fluorescence responses across the microspheres. Fig. 3f, displaying CLSM imagery at an excitation wavelength of 455 nm, illustrates how increasing CQD concentrations from CCUP-0.2 to CCUP-1.0 significantly enhances fluorescence intensity. This correlation highlights the capacity of CQDs to amplify fluorescence without altering the microspheres' visible coloration under standard lighting, as shown in the visual insets. Additionally, Fig. 3g presents fluorescence profiles at an excitation wavelength of 629 nm, which reveal the microspheres' adaptable fluorescence. This feature is instrumental for the advanced environmental monitoring tasks, such as the detection and analysis of contaminants. Together, these results underscore the dual functionality of the CCUP microspheres. They combine a consistent quantum dot distribution with adjustable fluorescence capabilities, making them exceptionally suited for their intended roles in both the detection and photocatalytic remediation of aqueous pollutants, notably hexavalent chromium.

The effects of UiO-66-NH2/PSF on the removal efficiency and degradation kinetics of Cr(VI) across various pH conditions were meticulously examined, as illustrated in Fig. 4. The removal efficiency of Cr(VI) exhibited a pronounced dependence on the pH of the solution (Fig. 4a). At pH∼2, the removal efficiency surpassed 80 %, whereas it diminished markedly with increasing pH, achieving only 14.67 % at pH∼7. This trend highlights the pivotal role of acidic conditions in augmenting Cr(VI) removal efficiency. The temporal change in Cr(VI) concentration at different pH values is depicted in Fig. 4b. At pH∼2, a rapid decline in Cr(VI) concentration is observed, indicative of efficient degradation kinetics. Conversely, at higher pH values (pH 5, 7, and 9), the reduction in Cr(VI) concentration occurs at a much slower rate, demonstrating diminished degradation efficacy. The kinetic rate constants for the photocatalytic degradation of Cr(VI) by UiO-66-NH2/PSF microspheres under different pH values are presented in Fig. 4c. The data reveal that the degradation rate is maximized at pH∼2, with a discernible linear relationship, suggesting that acidic conditions significantly enhance degradation kinetics. At elevated pH values, the degradation rate constants decrease, indicating reduced Cr(VI) removal efficiency.

The influence of different pH values on Cr(VI) removal efficiency is attributed to the varying ionic forms of Cr(VI). In aqueous solutions, Cr(VI) predominantly exists as HCrO4− and Cr2O7 2− when the pH is below 6, and as CrO4 2− when the pH is above 6. The increased concentration of H+ at lower pH levels can facilitate the protonation of amines on UiO-66-NH2, thereby enhancing the removal rate of Cr2O7 2−. Additionally, acidic media, owing to their higher redox potential, are favorable for the spontaneous reduction of Cr(VI) to Cr(III). As the pH increases (>7), a substantial presence of OH− in the solution leads to competition between the chromate ions (CrO4 2−) and OH− ions for adsorption sites on the adsorbent surfaces. Furthermore, part of Cr(VI) is reduced to Cr(III), which can precipitate as chromium hydroxide (Cr(OH)3). Compared with Cr(VI) species, flocculates such as chromium hydroxide are less readily adsorbed. Consequently, under alkaline conditions, the Cr(VI) elimination rates decline. Therefore, subsequent experiments targeting Cr(VI) elimination were conducted at a pH of approximately 2.

Fig. 5a demonstrates the correlation between porosity and density in the series of CCUP-x microspheres. The accompanying histogram demonstrates a consistent trend: despite an increase in CQD content, there is no significant alteration in the porosity and density of the CCUP-x microspheres. Notably, the densities of these microspheres are all below 1.0 g/cm³, enabling them to float on water. This buoyancy enhances their interaction with reactants and ensures more uniform distribution of released fluorescence. Experimental results verify that these microspheres exhibit outstanding buoyancy in water. For example, CCUP-0.5 features a density of about 0.92 g/cm³. This low density, along with significant electrostatic repulsion, aids in their stable flotation and enhances their reactivity in water-based environments.

Fig. 5b delineates the pore size distribution of the CCUP-x composite microspheres, employing mercury intrusion porosimetry to ascertain their structural characteristics. The resulting distribution is markedly narrow, as evinced by the sharp peak in the log differential intrusion graph, indicating a homogenous pore structure. The intrusion volume reaches 1.3 mL/g, with the total pore area extending to 49.7 m2/g, demonstrating the material's significant porosity. Median pore diameters are discerned as 18,954.86 nm when measured by volume and 12.32 nm when calculated by area, reflecting the precision-engineered pore sizes designed for specific functional applications. The graphical representation of mercury penetration effectively visualizes the porous architecture, confirming the quantitative data. An impressive porosity rate of 61.0 % is calculated, highlighting the extensive void fraction that enhances the microspheres' utility in catalytic and adsorptive environmental applications [50].

In the investigation of photocatalytic degradation of Cr(VI), the efficacy of polysulfone-based microspheres, coated with varying concentrations of carbon quantum dots and UiO-66-NH2 (CCUP-x), was rigorously assessed. As depicted in Fig. 5c, the photocatalytic activity of these composites significantly surpassed that of the unmodified UiO-66-NH2/PSF, particularly with the CCUP-1.0 composition achieving a 100 % removal efficiency of Cr(VI), a substantial increase by a factor of 1.3. Preceding light exposure, the CCUP microspheres adsorbed approximately 9 % of Cr(VI), highlighting their capability for dark adsorption. This potent adsorption characteristic is evident in the dark-adaptation phase of the experiment, with a noticeable decrease in Cr(VI) concentration, attributable to the microspheres' porous structure. Moreover, the highest catalytic degradation rate was observed at 0.29 mg·L−1·min−1 for the CCUP-1.0 microspheres. Kinetic analyses further validate the superior photocatalytic performance of the CCUP composites, as demonstrated in Fig. 5d. Fitting a pseudo-first-order degradation model, the CCUP-1.0 variant exhibited an impressive reaction rate constant of 0.00851 min−1, significantly more efficient than the original UiO-66-NH2/PSF at 0.01412 min−1 and the control photolysis rate at 0.003612 min−1. These findings not only underscore the exceptional catalytic proficiency of the CCUP microspheres in degrading Cr(VI) contaminants but also reflect their potential for application in real-world environmental remediation [51].

Fig. 5e showcases the photocatalytic degradation efficacy of CCUP-1.0 microspheres for removing Cr(VI) across five successive cycles. The degradation curves plotted indicate that the ratio Ct/ C0—which represents the relative concentration of Cr(VI) at time t against the initial concentration C0—only marginally diminishes, maintaining a degradation rate that consistently exceeds 98 % even after five cycles. This sustained performance, achieved without any mechanical agitation, highlights the robust and enduring photocatalytic capabilities of the microspheres. Fig. 5f further underscores the consistent recovery rate of the microspheres across multiple cycles, reinforcing their potential for repeated use in the decontamination of Cr(VI) ions. An inset in Fig. 5f compares the dark adsorption to the photocatalytic activity, where a slight decline in photocatalytic degradation is noted, possibly due to the progressive inactivation of catalytic sites over successive cycles [50,52]. Additionally, SEM images (referenced in Fig. S3) taken before and after the first and fifth cycles visually confirm that there is negligible loss of surface nanoparticles, thereby demonstrating the good structural integrity of the CCUP microspheres throughout repeated uses.

The XRD and XPS characterization data of CCUP-1.0 samples before and after 5 cycles of photocatalytic reaction elucidate changes in the materials' physical and chemical properties (Fig. S4). The XRD patterns (Fig. S4a) exhibit characteristic peaks at 2θ values corresponding to the planes (111), (002), (006), and (022), affirming the crystalline structure's stability and demonstrating its robustness and structural integrity. The XPS survey spectra (Fig. S4b) display core-level peaks for O 1 s, C 1 s, N 1 s, and Zr 3d, indicating the main elemental composition remains unaltered, thus signifying chemical stability. The high-resolution C 1 s spectra (Fig. S4c) present peaks for C–N, C–H/C–C, and O–CO bonds, with slight binding energy shifts after 5 cycles, attributable to minor surface chemical changes from interactions with Cr(VI) and the generation of intermediate species during the photocatalytic process. The high-resolution O 1 s spectra (Fig. 4d) reveal peaks for Olatt, Oads, and OOH, with slight shifts and intensity changes suggesting surface modifications due to hydroxyl radical formation and reaction intermediate adsorption. The high-resolution Zr 3d spectra (Fig. S4e) show consistent peaks for Zr 3d5/2 and Zr 3d3/2 before and after the reaction, confirming the stability of the Zr-based framework within UiO-66-NH2 during photocatalytic cycles. These analyses collectively indicate that CCUP-1.0 microspheres maintain their structural and chemical integrity after multiple photocatalytic cycles, underscoring their potential for long-term environmental remediation applications where durability and sustained performance are paramount.

Ultraviolet-visible light absorption spectroscopy was employed to further elucidate the role of CQD, as depicted in Fig. 6a. Compared to the baseline UiO-66-NH2, the CQD@UiO-66-NH2 composite exhibited enhanced absorption in the visible spectrum. However, a reduction in ultraviolet absorption was observed for the CQD@UiO-66-NH2 sample, likely due to the shielding effect of the CQDs. Notably, CQD presents a broad ultraviolet-visible light absorption band, peaking around 443.6 nm; this peak is substantially heightened in the CCU, indicating successful integration with PSF to form a novel material with augmented visible light absorption properties. This improvement adjusts the bandgap to better suit visible light, significantly enhancing the photocatalyst’s reactivity to visible light, thereby boosting the photocatalytic performance of the CCUP composite microspheres [53]. Fig. 6b illustrates the bandgaps (Eg) of various materials derived from their absorption curves. This analysis shows that incorporating CQDs not only noticeably reduces the bandgap of UiO-66-NH2 but also effectively elevates the HOMO (Highest Occupied Molecular Orbital) energy level of CCU, thereby enhancing its photocatalytic performance. These modifications facilitate the absorption of a broader spectrum of visible light, crucial for optimizing photocatalytic efficiency.

Fig. 6c demonstrates the transient photocurrent responses of CCUP-x composites under visible light irradiation, reflecting their photoelectrochemical activities. The response exhibits pronounced photocurrent peaks in a periodic fashion corresponding to the light source's on/off cycles. This indicates an efficient charge carrier generation and suggests a reduction in the recombination rate, particularly as the CQD content within the composites increases from 0.2 wt% to 1.0 wt%. The enhanced photocurrent with higher CQD content implies an improvement in photoactivity, which is beneficial for photocatalytic applications [54]. Fig. 6d's Nyquist plot further corroborates these findings, where the CCUP-1.0 composite shows a reduced semicircle radius, indicative of lower interfacial charge transfer resistance, hence suggesting enhanced electron transfer efficiency. The electrochemical behavior of CCUP-x composites, demonstrated by both transient photocurrent measurements and EIS, supports their effectiveness as photocatalysts. Notably, at a catalyst loading of 0.5 g/L, the CCUP series exhibited a full degradation efficiency for Cr(VI), with a rate constant of 0.02768 min−1, surpassing the performance of comparative photocatalysts [55]. These attributes confirm the CCUP-x composites as powerful candidates for the photodegradation of Cr(VI) in water treatment applications, with the potential for practical deployment in environmental remediation efforts.

Figs. 6e and 6f illuminate the detection and quantitation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) throughout the photocatalytic process in Fin CCUP-x composites via ESR spectroscopy, employing DMPO as a spin trap. The spectra unequivocally delineate the engenderment of hydroxyl (·OH) and superoxide (·O2-) radicals, fundamental agents in photocatalytic degradation, as evidenced by their characteristic ESR signals. The intensity of these signals is proportionally augmented with increasing concentrations of carbon quantum dots within the composites, demonstrating an enhanced photocatalytic activity as the CQD content rises from 0.2 wt% to 1.0 wt%. The highest intensities for DMPO-·OH and DMPO-·O2- signals observed in the CCUP-1.0 composite underline the significant role of CQD concentration in regulating ROS production and stabilization, which is critical for the efficient degradation of organic pollutants. These ESR results not only demonstrate the active participation of ROS in the photocatalytic degradation facilitated by CCUP-x composites but also suggest that these materials can be finely tuned by adjusting the CQD content, optimizing their performance for specific environmental applications [56].

Table 1 provides a comprehensive comparison of various advanced photocatalysts for Cr(VI) degradation from aqueous solutions, detailing contaminant concentrations, catalyst dosages, light sources, and reduction efficiencies within specific timeframes. The novel CCUP-1.0 catalyst highlighted in this study demonstrates an exceptional reduction efficiency of 99 % for a 20 mg/L Cr(VI) solution, utilizing a catalyst dosage of 20 mg/L under a 350 W Xenon lamp within 135 min. This performance surpasses benchmarks set by photocatalysts such as NNU-36, MIL-68(In)-NH2, and various carbon nitride and bismuth tungstate composites, which exhibit efficiencies ranging from 66 % to 98 % under similar conditions. The superior efficacy of CCUP-1.0 is attributed to several key factors: enhanced light utilization through the incorporation of CQDs into UiO-66-NH2, which broadens light absorption and increases reactive oxygen species generation; efficient electron-hole separation facilitated by its unique structure, reducing recombination rates and enhancing free radical availability for Cr(VI) reduction; optimal catalyst dosage and light source, with the 350 W Xenon lamp providing high-intensity light that maximizes photocatalytic efficiency; robust structural characteristics, as the hollow PSF microspheres embedded with quantum dots and MOFs increase the surface area available for reactions, leading to higher adsorption and degradation rates; and high selectivity and stability, with CCUP-1.0 microspheres exhibiting high selectivity for Cr(VI) over other metal ions and demonstrating stability across multiple usage cycles. These attributes collectively establish CCUP-1.0 as a leading material for the photoremediation of aqueous systems contaminated with heavy metal ions.

Fig. 7 presents the mechanism underlying the photocatalytic oxidation-reduction degradation of Cr(VI) by CCUP microspheres. The energy diagram illustrates the electronic structure and photocatalytic processes that occur when the microspheres are exposed to visible light. The bandgap energy, the positions of the valence and conduction bands, and the main reactive species involved suggest a precise mechanistic pathway. In this system, under visible light irradiation, electrons in the LUMO level of UiO-66-NH2 are excited to the CQDs. This results in the generation of electron-hole pairs where the photo-excited electrons from the conduction band of CQDs are transferred to the conduction band of CdTeQDs, facilitating the reduction of Cr(VI) to Cr(III). The valence band holes in the CQDs interact with water molecules or hydroxide ions to produce hydroxyl radicals (·OH), potent oxidants in the degradation process. Notably, the Cr(VI) ions introduce new defect levels within the band structure of the CdTeQDs, which can trap the photo-generated carriers, thus increasing the electron-hole recombination efficiency and subsequently affecting the photoluminescence efficiency of the CdTeQDs. Due to the high electron affinity of Cr(VI), these ions can act as effective electron acceptors, causing the electrons from CdTeQDs to transfer to Cr(VI). This electron transfer process leads to the quenching of photoluminescence in CdTeQDs because the excited electrons are rapidly captured by Cr(VI) ions, preventing them from recombining with holes and emitting light. Subsequently, the holes in the VB of UiO-66-NH2 and CQDs can oxidize water molecules or hydroxide ions (OH-), producing hydroxyl radicals (·OH). Hydroxyl radicals (·OH) are highly active and contribute to the degradation of organic pollutants, further assisting in photocatalytic reduction processes.

The process is significantly enhanced due to the spatial separation of charge carriers within the CCUP microspheres, which extends the lifetime of the excited electrons and holes. This extended lifespan augments the probability of these carriers participating in surface redox reactions. As such, the intricate interplay of component interactions within the CCUP microspheres effectively amplifies the photocatalytic degradation and photoreduction capacity under visible light irradiation. Through this detailed elucidation, the proposed mechanism accentuates the integrated functions of the individual constituents of the CCUP microspheres. The UiO-66-NH2 acts as an electron transfer conduit and a structural scaffold, the CQDs facilitate charge separation, and the CdTeQDs serve both as an electron source for Cr(VI) reduction and as a sensory agent for the defect-induced photoluminescence quenching. The collective synergy of these components is pivotal for the enhanced photocatalytic activity of the CCUP system, offering a robust approach to not only degrade Cr(VI) efficiently but also to monitor the process in real-time through fluorescence signal variation. This insight into the mechanism of action provides a substantial foundation for the application of CCUP microspheres in environmental remediation of heavy metal ions.

In this study, we have demonstrated the successful development of CCUP composite microspheres, exhibiting a persistent photocatalytic degradation efficiency over 98 % for Cr(VI) across five cycles, underscoring their potential for sustainable water treatment applications. The structural resilience of these microspheres is confirmed through SEM analyses, which revealed minimal loss of surface nanoparticles, indicative of their robustness after multiple degradation cycles. Fluorescence measurements indicate a significant quenching of Cr(VI) at 629 nm, establishing the microspheres as a reliable sensor with a high degree of selectivity for Cr(VI) over other metal ions. The microspheres' buoyancy—attributable to their low density of approximately 0.92 g/cm³ for CCUP-0.5—along with a porosity rate of 61.0011 % as quantified by mercury intrusion porosimetry, substantially enhances their photocatalytic interaction and substance exchange capabilities. Photocatalytic activity assessments under simulated sunlight conditions revealed a 1.3-fold increase in the Cr(VI) removal rate when compared to UiO-66-NH2/PSF. Electron spin resonance spectroscopy confirmed the generation of reactive oxygen species, essential for effective photocatalysis. Furthermore, the notable 204 nm separation between the dual-emission peaks of the CCUP microspheres minimizes signal interference, optimizing the detection process. The data presented herein reinforce the viability of CCUP microspheres as a practical and efficient material for the photodegradation of Cr(VI), presenting a valuable contribution to the field of environmental remediation. Future research will focus on optimizing these materials for broader environmental applications, scaling up production, and extending their pollutant removal capabilities to ensure cleaner water resources on a global scale.
